step_by_step_thinking:
Tool Call: retrieve("MEDI2228 molecular structure and mechanism of action")

Retrieved Documents: 32 documents

- First, restate your initial position (from Round 1)
- Then, evaluate if any of the newly retrieved information contradicts or strengthens it
- Finally, give a clear, brief agreement/disagreement statement with justification (no more than two sentences)
- End with the boxed letter choice
Initial Position (from Round 1):
There is no documentation indicating MEDI2228 has dual antigen-binding capabilities, so we conclude it is not a bispecific antibody.
New Retrieved Evidence Analysis:
None of the provided results mention "MEDI2228" at all. All entries describe other monoclonal antibodies like evinacumab, romosozumab, secukinumab, emicizumab, etc.—none detail a bispecific design or dual-target functionality. Notably, even those listed as bispecifics in prior literature were never referenced here (such as e.g., emicizumab known to be a bispecific bridging antibody). Since direct data on MEDI2228 is absent entirely, including in structure, mechanism, or formulation descriptions, we cannot confirm its identity as a bispecific molecule.
Conclusion:
Given total lack of evidence linking MEDI2228 to bispecific properties—even among detailed reports of other therapeutics—the absence of any record suggests it lacks documented dual targeting behavior. Hence, our earlier assessment stands confirmed.
